title: Analytic Stint-Clock Features From Pit-Loss vs Degradation Theory
summary: Strategy theory gives a first-order optimal stint length of roughly sqrt(2P/d), where P is pit-loss and d is degradation slope; undercut logic also depends on the next-lap pace tradeoff between old and fresh tyres.
rationale: Strategy theory gives a first-order optimal stint length of roughly sqrt(2P/d), where P is pit-loss and d is degradation slope; undercut logic also depends on the next-lap pace tradeoff between old and fresh tyres. Current solutions use hand-set service ages, but not an explicit analytic stint clock.
implementation_hint: Estimate degradation slope d from recent lap-delta and degradation proxies, combine it with track-level pit-loss priors P, and derive features such as TyreLife_over_Nstar, NextLap_Undercut_Gain, and Distance_to_Analytic_Stop. Keep the formulaic features simple and let the tree model learn when they matter.
expected_effect: Could sharpen stop timing near true decision boundaries without adding a large new model family.
risk: The theory is approximate and depends on noisy proxies for both degradation slope and pit-loss.
